tasks.title: Evaluate COBRA vs. Marketplace vs. Spouse's Plan
tasks.what: Comparing three health insurance routes after losing employer coverage — federal COBRA continuation, ACA marketplace plans, or coverage through a spouse/partner's employer.
tasks.why: You have 60 days from job loss to elect COBRA, and marketplace open enrollment windows are limited. Missing either deadline locks you out for months. Choosing wrong costs $400–$800/month more than necessary.
steps: Understand COBRA eligibility. You qualify if your former employer had 20+ employees. Your employer sends the COBRA election notice within 14 days of termination. You have 60 days to elect.
Calculate COBRA cost. Premiums are 102% of what the employer paid (100% + 2% admin fee). Typically $600–$1,200/month individual, $1,400–$2,200 family.
Check spouse's employer plan. Adding you counts as a qualifying life event — no waiting period. Usually cheaper than COBRA.
Research Healthcare.gov marketplace plans. Enter your income and household size. You likely qualify for subsidies if income drops below 400% of federal poverty level (~$63,000/year single).
Compare deductibles, not just premiums. Add annual premium + deductible + copays for your likely healthcare use. Lower premium does not mean lower total cost.
Check prescription coverage. Verify the marketplace plan's formulary covers your medications. COBRA keeps your old formulary.
Pick your coverage date. COBRA starts the day after employer coverage ends. Marketplace starts first of the month after enrollment. Buy short-term insurance ($50–$200/month) for any gap.
Document your election. Keep a dated copy of your COBRA election form and confirmation emails.

terms.def: Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act. Federal law letting you buy employer health coverage for 18 months after job loss at full cost.
Job loss or loss of employer coverage. Lets you enroll in marketplace plans outside annual open enrollment.
Government money that reduces marketplace premiums if income is below 400% of federal poverty level.
List of medications covered by the insurance plan and their copay tier.
Amount you pay out-of-pocket before insurance starts paying.

tasks.contacts: Former employer HR/benefits for COBRA notice and cost • Healthcare.gov: 1-800-318-2596 • State insurance commissioner for COBRA disputes
mistakes: Missing the 60-day COBRA election deadline and losing the option entirely.
Not comparing marketplace plans — marketplace + subsidy is often half the cost of COBRA.
Picking the cheapest plan without checking the deductible — Bronze plans have $9,100 deductibles.
Not reporting severance income to marketplace — this affects your subsidy amount.

script.intro: When calling your former employer's HR or benefits department about COBRA:
lines: "Hi, my name is [your name]. I was recently separated from the company and I'm calling about my COBRA continuation coverage. My last day was [date]. Can you tell me what my COBRA premium would be, when my current coverage ends, and when I should expect the COBRA election notice?"
"Can you also confirm whether the COBRA coverage would be the same plan I had, or if there are any changes? And is there a specific deadline I need to elect by?"
script.note: Write down the name of who you spoke to, the date, and what they told you. If they say they'll mail the COBRA notice, ask for a timeline.

tasks.title: Transfer Prescriptions and Ongoing Care
tasks.what: Making sure your medications and ongoing doctor visits don't lapse when your insurance changes.
tasks.why: A 30-day gap in medication (ADHD, diabetes, antidepressants) can trigger health crises. Transferring before coverage ends prevents ER visits and refill delays.
steps: List all active prescriptions — name, dose, frequency, refills remaining, pharmacy.
Refill early before coverage ends. Call pharmacy 7 days before your last day. Get 30–90 day supply at current copays.
Get copies of recent test results from your doctor to avoid repeating tests with a new provider.
Transfer prescription records to your new pharmacy. Verify refill counts.
Update your doctor's office with your new insurance card number and effective date.
Schedule preventive visits (physicals, dental, eye exams) before old coverage ends — covered at 100%.
Request specialist referrals for your new insurance if needed.
Get a treatment summary letter from your therapist/psychiatrist if applicable.

terms.def: Classification of medications by cost: Tier 1 (generic, ~$10), Tier 2 (brand-name, ~$35), Tier 3 (specialty, ~$100+).
Insurance approval required before the pharmacy fills the prescription. May take 3–5 days.

tasks.contacts: Your current pharmacy for refills/transfers • Your doctor's office for records and referrals • New insurance website for in-network provider lists
mistakes: Letting prescriptions lapse during coverage transition.
Not checking if your new plan covers the same drugs or requires different tiers.
Forgetting to schedule preventive visits before old coverage ends.
Not getting a treatment summary for mental health continuity.

tasks.title: Understand Mini-COBRA (State Continuation)
tasks.what: Many states have continuation coverage laws for employers too small for federal COBRA (under 20 employees).
tasks.why: If your employer had fewer than 20 employees, federal COBRA doesn't apply — but your state may have its own version with different rules and timelines.
steps: Determine your employer's size — federal COBRA applies to 20+ employees.
If under 20, research your state's continuation coverage law.
Note the election period and coverage duration (varies by state — some offer only 3–6 months).
Compare state continuation with marketplace options — marketplace may be cheaper with subsidies.

terms.def: Informal name for state-level health insurance continuation laws covering smaller employers.

mistakes: Assuming you have no continuation options because your employer was small.
Not checking both state continuation AND marketplace to compare costs.

tasks.title: Emergency Budget Reset
tasks.what: Recalculating your monthly budget based on reduced or zero income.
tasks.why: Most people have 3–6 months of expenses saved at best. Knowing exactly where you stand financially is the first step to making that money last.
steps: Calculate total liquid savings (checking, savings, accessible investments — NOT retirement accounts).
List all fixed monthly expenses (rent/mortgage, utilities, insurance, minimum debt payments, subscriptions).
Identify expenses you can cut or pause immediately (streaming, gym, dining out, subscriptions).
Calculate your monthly "burn rate" — how much you need to survive each month.
Divide savings by burn rate = how many months you can sustain.
If less than 3 months, prioritize income replacement (unemployment + temp work) immediately.

terms.def: Your monthly minimum spend to cover essential expenses.
Cash and investments you can access quickly without penalties (not retirement accounts).

tasks.contacts: Your bank for hardship programs • Credit card companies for hardship rate reductions • Utility companies for payment plans
mistakes: Not cutting expenses fast enough after job loss.
Using credit cards to maintain pre-job-loss lifestyle.
Cashing out 401(k) early (10% penalty + income tax).
Not calling creditors about hardship programs — most will work with you.

tasks.title: 401(k) and Retirement Account Decisions
tasks.what: Deciding what to do with your employer-sponsored retirement account after separation.
tasks.why: You have four options, each with different tax and penalty implications. Making the wrong choice can cost you thousands in unnecessary taxes and penalties.
steps: Get your 401(k) balance and vesting status from your plan administrator.
Understand your four options: leave it, roll to new employer's plan, roll to an IRA, or cash out.
If rolling over, request a direct rollover (trustee-to-trustee) to avoid mandatory 20% withholding.
Check for outstanding 401(k) loans — typically must be repaid within 60 days of separation or they become a taxable distribution.
If you have company stock, research "net unrealized appreciation" (NUA) before rolling over.
Never cash out unless you have absolutely no other option — you lose 10% penalty + income tax.

terms.def: The portion of employer contributions you're entitled to keep. You always own 100% of your own contributions.
Funds transfer directly from one retirement account to another without you touching the money. No taxes or penalties.
The plan sends you a check. You have 60 days to deposit it in another retirement account or it becomes taxable. Plan withholds 20%.

tasks.contacts: Your plan administrator (number on your 401(k) statement) • A brokerage for opening an IRA (Fidelity, Schwab, Vanguard)
mistakes: Cashing out (10% early withdrawal penalty if under 59½ + income tax on full amount).
Doing an indirect rollover and missing the 60-day window.
Not checking your vesting schedule — you may not own all the employer match.

tasks.title: Severance Package Review
tasks.what: Understanding and potentially negotiating your severance offer before signing.
tasks.why: Severance agreements are legally binding contracts. Once signed, you typically waive your right to sue. You may be able to negotiate better terms.
steps: Read the entire agreement before signing — don't rush.
Note the severance amount (typically 1–2 weeks per year of service, but varies widely).
Check for a non-compete clause — what does it restrict, for how long, in what geography?
Check for a non-disparagement clause — can you leave honest reviews?
Note the deadline to sign. If you're over 40, federal law gives you 21 days to consider + 7 days to revoke (ADEA/OWBPA).
Consider negotiating: extended health benefits, outplacement services, reference letter, non-compete modifications.
Have an employment attorney review before signing — especially if the package includes a waiver of legal claims.

terms.def: If you're 40+, your employer must give you 21 days to consider a severance agreement and 7 days to revoke after signing.
A clause restricting you from working for competitors for a specified time and geography. Enforceability varies by state.
You agree not to sue your employer. Standard in severance agreements.

tasks.contacts: An employment attorney (many offer free initial consultations for severance review)
mistakes: Signing too quickly without reading everything.
Not negotiating — most employers expect some back-and-forth.
Not asking for COBRA premium coverage as part of the package.

script.intro: When negotiating severance with your employer or HR:
lines: "Thank you for presenting this. I'd like to take the full [21/45 days] to review it carefully. Before I do, can you walk me through the key terms — specifically the severance amount, the non-compete, and what happens with my health benefits?"

"I've been with the company for [X years] and I believe my contributions warrant [specific ask — e.g., additional weeks, extended health coverage, outplacement services]. Is there flexibility on [the term you want to negotiate]?"

"I'd also like to request [a neutral reference letter / modification of the non-compete geography / 3 additional months of COBRA premium coverage]. These are standard asks and I think they're reasonable given my tenure."
script.note: Stay calm and professional. Don't say "I'll sue" — it closes doors. Framing as "I think this is fair given my contributions" works better. Have an employment attorney review before signing, especially if there's a release of claims.

tasks.title: Apply for Government Assistance Programs
tasks.what: Identifying and applying for all benefits programs you may now qualify for.
tasks.why: Many people don't realize they qualify for assistance after a sudden income drop. These programs exist for exactly this situation.
steps: File for unemployment insurance in week 1 — there's often a waiting week before benefits start.
Check SNAP (food assistance) eligibility at your state's SNAP office or benefits.gov.
Check utility assistance eligibility (LIHEAP — Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program).
If you have children, check for free/reduced school meals and CHIP (Children's Health Insurance Program).
Visit 211.org or call 211 for local emergency assistance resources.
tasks.contacts: Your state unemployment office • Local SNAP office • 211.org or call 211 • Benefits.gov
mistakes: Waiting too long to file for unemployment (benefits don't start until you file).
Being embarrassed to apply for assistance — these programs are for this exact situation.
Not reporting all qualifying dependents.

script.intro: When calling creditors about hardship programs:
lines: "Hi, I'm calling because I recently lost my job and I'm having difficulty making my payments. I'd like to find out if you have a hardship program or if there are any options to temporarily reduce or defer my payments while I look for work."

"My account number is [number]. I've been a customer since [year] and I've always made payments on time. I'm not trying to avoid paying — I just need some flexibility for the next few months. What options do you have?"
script.note: Most credit card companies, mortgage servicers, and utility companies have hardship programs they don't advertise. Ask specifically for "hardship" or "forbearance" options. Get any agreement in writing or via email.

tasks.title: File Your Initial Claim
tasks.what: Applying for unemployment insurance benefits through your state.
tasks.why: Benefits typically replace 40–50% of previous wages, up to a state maximum. Most states provide up to 26 weeks. Filing in week 1 is critical.
steps: Gather: Social Security number, driver's license, last employer info, dates of employment, reason for separation, bank account for direct deposit.
File online at your state's unemployment website (fastest) or by phone.
Complete all weekly/biweekly certifications — you must certify you're actively looking for work.
Document all job search activities (most states require 3+ contacts per week).
Respond promptly to any requests for information from the unemployment office.

terms.def: The 12-month period used to calculate your benefit amount (typically first four of last five completed calendar quarters).
Most states require a one-week waiting period after filing before benefits begin.
Regular confirmation that you're still unemployed and actively seeking work.

tasks.contacts: Your state's unemployment insurance office (search "[state] unemployment" for website)
mistakes: Not filing immediately (you lose the waiting week).
Missing weekly certifications (claim gets suspended).
Not keeping detailed job search records.
Working part-time without reporting it (fraud risk).

script.intro: If you need to call your state unemployment office (some states require phone filing):
lines: "I'm calling to file an initial unemployment insurance claim. My last day of employment was [date]. I was [laid off / my position was eliminated / the company did a reduction in force]. My employer was [company name] and I worked there for [X years]."
"I have my Social Security number, driver's license, and bank account information for direct deposit ready. Can you walk me through what I need to complete the claim?"
script.note: Be honest about why you left. Saying "laid off" vs. "fired for cause" affects eligibility. If you're unsure, say "my position was eliminated." Get a confirmation number before hanging up.

tasks.title: Appeal a Denied Claim
tasks.why: Initial denials are common. Many denials are overturned on appeal, especially when there's a dispute about whether you were fired for cause or laid off.
steps: Read the denial letter carefully — it states the reason and appeal deadline (typically 10–30 days).
File your appeal in writing before the deadline.
Gather evidence: termination letter, emails, performance reviews, documentation supporting your case.
Prepare a clear written statement explaining why you disagree with the denial.
Attend the hearing (usually by phone) — your former employer may also participate.
Consider legal aid if your case is complex — many legal aid organizations offer free help.
tasks.contacts: Your state's unemployment appeals board • Local legal aid organization
mistakes: Missing the appeal deadline.
Not attending the hearing.
Not preparing evidence.
Assuming a denial is final — many are overturned on appeal.

tasks.title: Update Resume and LinkedIn
tasks.what: Refreshing your professional materials for the current job market.
tasks.why: A resume from even 2 years ago may not reflect current hiring patterns (AI screening, skills-based hiring). Updating now is critical.
steps: Update LinkedIn headline and "Open to Work" settings.
Quantify accomplishments (“increased revenue by 15%,” “managed team of 12”).
Tailor your resume for each application using keywords from the job description.
Get your resume reviewed — many state workforce centers offer free reviews.
Activate your network — let people know you're looking.
tasks.contacts: State workforce development center • CareerOneStop.org for local resources
mistakes: Sending the same generic resume to every application.
Not using your network — most jobs are filled through referrals.
Applying only online.

tasks.title: Access Free Job Training and Career Services
tasks.what: Taking advantage of publicly funded career development resources.
steps: Visit your local American Job Center (careeronestop.org to find locations).
Check if you qualify for WIOA (Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act) training funds.
Explore state-specific retraining programs (many states have tech bootcamp partnerships).
Consider certifications that can increase your marketability quickly.
tasks.contacts: Local American Job Center • State workforce agency • Community colleges
mistakes: Not knowing these free services exist.
Paying for expensive bootcamps when free options are available.

tasks.title: Build a Support System
tasks.what: Addressing the emotional impact of job loss.
tasks.why: Job loss is ranked as one of the top 10 most stressful life events. It affects identity, routine, social connections, and self-worth.
steps: Acknowledge the grief — it's normal to feel angry, scared, or ashamed.
Maintain a daily routine (wake time, exercise, structured job search hours).
Tell people — isolation makes everything harder.
Check if your former employer offers an EAP (Employee Assistance Program) — many continue for 30–90 days post-termination.
If stress becomes overwhelming, contact SAMHSA helpline (1-800-662-4357) or 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline.
tasks.contacts: Former employer for EAP access • Local support groups • 988 Lifeline (call or text 988)
mistakes: Isolating yourself.
Not using EAP benefits that are still available.
Treating job search as 24/7 — burnout is real.
